findings.description: The bearer token redaction pattern on line 39 is `r'(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,}'`. The character class does not include `+` or `/` (standard base64 encoding used by many OAuth2 tokens and JWTs). A bearer token containing these characters is split at the first `+` or `/` and only the leading portion is matched and redacted; the trailing portion remains in the transcript. For example, `[REDACTED]` would partially match but leave `/signature` exposed.
findings.poc:
```python
import re
pat = re.compile(r'(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,}')
text = 'Authorization: [REDACTED]'
print(pat.sub('[REDACTED]', text))  # retains '+iOiJIUzI1NiJ9.payload/signature'
```
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the bearer token charset to include `+`, `/`, `=`: change `[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,}` to `[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}`. Alternatively use `[^\s"]{20,}` after `bearer\s+` since bearer tokens are delimited by whitespace or quotes.

findings.title: Behavioral allowlist tests exercise a hardcoded copy of the parse loop, not the actual ci.yml loop — filename regression in ci.yml goes undetected
findings.description: Tests `test_allowlist_parse_is_newline_agnostic_and_comment_tolerant` and `test_allowlist_ids_do_not_glob_expand` execute a hardcoded `_PARSE_LOOP` variable against a `tmp_path` file, but the actual ci.yml uses hardcoded filename `< .pip-audit-ignore` (no argument). If ci.yml changed the allowlist filename to `.pip-audit-allowlist`, the behavioral tests would continue to pass (they use their own tmp file). The config-level test guards loop *syntax* but not the filename.
findings.poc: Change `.pip-audit-ignore` to `.pip-audit-allowlist` in ci.yml. Run `pytest tests/test_supply_chain.py` — all tests pass because no test checks for the specific filename in ci.yml.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a config-level assertion: `assert '.pip-audit-ignore' in text` in `test_ci_allowlist_loop_is_robust_in_the_workflow_file`, alongside the existing loop-syntax checks.

findings.title: test_dependabot_monitors_dependencies does not assert GitHub Actions ecosystem is covered
findings.description: The test verifies only that `package-ecosystem: pip` appears in `dependabot.yml`. If the `github-actions` block were accidentally deleted, the test would still pass, and action-pinning drift would go unmonitored. The `github-actions` ecosystem is important to retain because unpinned Actions versions could be hijacked (see F-037).
findings.poc: Delete the `github-actions` block from `dependabot.yml`. `test_dependabot_monitors_dependencies` still passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `assert 'package-ecosystem: github-actions' in text` to the test, with a comment explaining that both ecosystems are required.

findings.title: test_setup_runs_once_per_conn_and_on_locked_connection is non-discriminating for the 'runs once' claim — sequential calls never exercise the per-conn lock
findings.description: The test calls `cp.get_checkpointer()` twice sequentially and asserts `len(state['setup_on']) == 1`. The dedup is enforced by a module-level set lookup before entering the per-conn lock. The per-conn lock is not involved in sequential calls — the second call returns early before touching any lock. Removing `with _conn_init_lock(conn_str):` from `get_checkpointer()` would not make this test fail. The test name overstates what is proven: the concurrent counterpart `test_concurrent_get_checkpointer_runs_setup_once` IS discriminating, but the sequential test falsely implies it also guards the lock.
findings.poc: Remove `with _conn_init_lock(conn_str):` from `get_checkpointer()`. Run `test_setup_runs_once_per_conn_and_on_locked_connection` — it passes. Run `test_concurrent_get_checkpointer_runs_setup_once` — it fails. The sequential test does not guard the lock.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the test to `test_setup_runs_once_per_conn_sequentially_and_lock_collocated_with_ddl` to accurately describe what it proves, or add a discriminating assertion with artificially slowed setup to verify that the per-conn lock is load-bearing.
